summary: PR #388 has one HIGH-severity correctness regression that confirms the Bugbot finding: convertLegacyMatchers in CachePolicyConfig silently drops legacy `finality` and `empty` fields when no Network/Method/Params are set, replacing them with a default catch-all that ignores those filters. A YAML like `{ connector: x, finality: realtime, ttl: 5s }` (cache only realtime data) becomes 'cache everything regardless of finality with 5s TTL' — semantics inverted, no warning. Same for `empty: only` (negative-cache-only configs become positive-cache configs with the OPPOSITE empty semantics). The existing test at common/config_test.go:1404 codifies this buggy behavior as expected. There is also a MEDIUM footgun: failsafe matchers with `empty: only` never select the executor (because executor selection runs with resp=nil → isEmptyish=false), and validation does not reject this misuse. The merge resolution itself is correct: combined-resolution in json_rpc_cache.go properly chains the matchers-PR PolicyWithMatcher iteration with main's shouldAcceptCachedResult age guard, getFailsafeExecutor's fallback-to-last semantics correctly returns the default catch-all executor (which networks_registry.go always appends), and SetDefaults() in NewCachePolicy is idempotent (early-return on len(Matchers)>0). matchers.Match has no internal cache, so the 'race conditions in matcher cache' concern in the prompt is a phantom — there's no shared mutable state.

findings.title: convertLegacyMatchers silently drops legacy `finality` and `empty` fields when Network/Method/Params are unset
findings.description:
In CachePolicyConfig.convertLegacyMatchers (common/defaults.go:622), the gate `hasLegacyFields := c.Network != "" || c.Method != "" || len(c.Params) > 0` excludes `c.Finality` and `c.Empty`. When a user writes a legacy config that only sets one of those two fields (e.g. `{ connector: x, finality: realtime, ttl: 5s }` or `{ connector: x, empty: only, ttl: 60s }`), `hasLegacyFields` is false → the legacy-conversion block at lines 626-657 is skipped → the fallback at line 660-665 appends a default catch-all `{Method: '*', Action: Include}` with NO Finality and Empty=Ignore (zero value). The user's filters are silently dropped.

Concrete reproducer (Go test):
```go
policy := &CachePolicyConfig{Connector: "x", Finality: DataFinalityStateRealtime}
policy.SetDefaults()
// policy.Matchers == [{Method:"*", Action:Include, Finality:nil, Empty:0}]
// User's `finality: realtime` filter is gone.
```

In pre-PR main (common/config.go and data/cache_policy.go MatchesForSet), the same input would have applied `p.config.Finality (Realtime) == request.Finality` — so only realtime requests would have matched. Now every request matches and gets cached with the realtime TTL.

The `empty` case is worse: the zero value CacheEmptyBehaviorIgnore (=0) is the OPPOSITE of CacheEmptyBehaviorOnly. So `{ connector: x, empty: only }` (a negative-cache-only policy) becomes effectively `{ empty: ignore }` (don't cache empty results) — caches the wrong things AND skips the things the user wanted to cache.

The existing test at common/config_test.go:1404-1413 ('empty legacy fields create default catch-all include matcher') asserts `policy.Matchers[0].Method == "*"` for `&CachePolicyConfig{}` — pinning the buggy behavior in place. There is no test for `&CachePolicyConfig{Finality: Realtime}` or `&CachePolicyConfig{Empty: Only}` alone, which is why the regression slipped through.
findings.impact: Silent semantic flip for legacy CachePolicyConfig users who relied solely on `finality` or `empty` filtering at the policy level. They will see (a) caching of data they did not want cached (e.g., finalized data with a realtime-tuned short TTL → unnecessary upstream churn for finalized queries that should hit a long-TTL policy), or (b) inverted negative-cache behavior (Empty:Only → Empty:Ignore caches non-empty responses and skips empties — opposite of intent). Bugs are hard to detect in testing because nothing throws — the cache just behaves wrong.
findings.recommendation:
Extend the gate to include non-zero finality and empty, OR (preferred) always synthesize a matcher when matchers is empty, copying every legacy field through:
```go
hasLegacyFields := c.Network != "" || c.Method != "" || len(c.Params) > 0 ||
    c.Finality != DataFinalityStateFinalized ||  // non-default
    c.Empty != CacheEmptyBehaviorIgnore           // non-default
```
Note `Finality: finalized` (zero value) cannot be distinguished from 'unset' without a schema change to *DataFinalityState — but that ambiguity already existed in main and a default catch-all with no finality (matches all) is semantically wider than main's strict equality, so the fallback default behavior is at least a permissive superset for the unset case. Add tests:
- `&CachePolicyConfig{Finality: DataFinalityStateRealtime}` → matcher.Finality == [Realtime].
- `&CachePolicyConfig{Empty: CacheEmptyBehaviorOnly}` → matcher.Empty == Only.
- `&CachePolicyConfig{Empty: CacheEmptyBehaviorAllow}` → matcher.Empty == Allow.
And update common/config_test.go:1404 to assert that empty CachePolicyConfig produces a no-finality matcher (i.e., document the NEW intended catch-all semantics).

findings.title: Failsafe matchers with `empty: only` or `empty: ignore` (non-zero) never match because executor selection runs with isEmptyish=false
findings.description: MatchConfig (matchers/matcher.go:51-57) uses the same `Empty` filter for both cache policy matching (where isEmptyish is computed from the response body) and failsafe executor selection (where matchers.Match is called with `resp=nil`, forcing isEmptyish=false at line 170-173). For a failsafe matcher with `empty: only`, MatchConfig hits `if !isEmptyish && config.Empty == CacheEmptyBehaviorOnly { return false }` → the matcher never matches → the executor is never selected during request routing. This is silent: validation in common/validation.go does not reject `empty` on failsafe matchers, and there is no warning at config load. A user pasting a copy of a cache matcher into a failsafe config (which uses the same MatcherConfig type) gets a dead executor.
findings.impact: Failsafe configs that mistakenly include `empty: only/allow` on matchers will route every request through the no-policy default executor (corr-related to rel-001 in the reliability review). Severity is moderate because the misuse is unusual, but it compounds the silent-fallthrough behavior of the new selection algorithm.
findings.recommendation: Add a validation-time rejection: when a MatcherConfig is attached to a FailsafeConfig (vs. a CachePolicyConfig), reject any non-default `Empty` value with a clear error like 'failsafe.matchers[i].empty is not supported (cache-only field)'. Or, alternatively, the simpler/safer fix: have matchers.Match skip the Empty check when called for failsafe selection (resp is nil) — but that requires distinguishing the call sites. The validation route is cleaner and faster to ship.

findings.title: Dead code in FailsafeConfig.convertLegacyMatchers post-conversion catch-all override
findings.description:
After the outer `if f.MatchMethod != "" || len(f.MatchFinality) > 0` block builds a matcher and appends it, the trailing block at lines 2186-2189:
```go
if len(f.Matchers) == 1 && f.Matchers[0].Method == "" && len(f.Matchers[0].Finality) == 0 {
    f.Matchers[0].Method = "*"
}
```
is unreachable. Reaching this point requires the outer condition to be true, which means at least one of `f.MatchMethod` (→ matcher.Method non-empty) or `f.MatchFinality` (→ matcher.Finality non-empty) was set. So `Method == ""` AND `len(Finality) == 0` cannot both hold. Not a correctness bug, but it implies an intended invariant that the code never enforces.
findings.impact: None at runtime; pure clarity/maintenance concern. A future reader may assume there is a defensive case being handled and hesitate to refactor.
findings.recommendation: Delete the block, or move the catch-all-defaulting logic into Normalize() where it actually runs (the 'len(f.Matchers) == 0 → append catch-all' branch already covers the no-legacy-fields case correctly).

residual_risks: PR description and the merge-resolution comment in upstream defaults (common/defaults.go:1676-1677) explicitly note: 'matchers PR (#388) deliberately replaced per-method/finality merging with user policies override defaults entirely.' Existing users whose configs relied on default failsafe entries supplementing their per-method overrides will see uncovered methods fall through to the no-policy default executor. This is intentional (and corr-003-related to rel-001) but the silent skip of TestSetDefaults_NetworkConfig_FailsafeMatchMethod (10 subtests) means the new defaulting behavior has reduced regression coverage. Recommend adding replacement tests that exercise the matcher-based defaulting before merging.

MatchesForGetWithMatcher (data/cache_policy.go:129-204) does NOT use matchers.MatchConfig — it has its own iteration loop with special finality semantics ('Finalized requests can match both Finalized and Unfinalized policies'). This intentional asymmetry preserves main's GET behavior, but it means any future change to MatchConfig's finality logic must also be replicated in MatchesForGetWithMatcher or the two will silently diverge. Worth a comment cross-referencing the two functions; absence of one is a code-smell that could trigger a future regression.

matchers.Match swallows req.JsonRpcRequest() errors (matchers/matcher.go:165-167); on error, params stays nil. A matcher with `params:` constraints will then fail to match → executor falls through to default. Silent under the hood but follows the standard 'matcher doesn't match → fall through' contract; document it explicitly so future readers don't think params-based matching is broken when methods like net_version (no params) hit the matcher.

Validation prepends a catch-all when a matchers list has only one polarity (only includes → prepend exclude; only excludes → prepend include). Combined with last-takes-precedence iteration, user matchers correctly take priority over the auto-prepended catch-all. But if validation runs twice on the same FailsafeConfig (e.g. a programmatic test or a hot-reload code path), the second call sees mixed include+exclude and goes through the strict 'first rule must be a catch-all' check; this passes for the auto-prepended catch-all but would reject any user config that sets the first rule with non-default Network/Method/Params/Finality. Idempotency is intact today (LoadConfig only validates once), but a future hot-reload path needs to guard against double validation.
testing_gaps: No test covers `&CachePolicyConfig{Finality: DataFinalityStateRealtime}` alone (only-finality-set case). Add to common/config_test.go's TestCachePolicySetDefaults: assert that the resulting matcher preserves Finality == [Realtime] after SetDefaults. Same for Unfinalized and Unknown.

No test covers `&CachePolicyConfig{Empty: CacheEmptyBehaviorOnly}` alone (only-empty-set case). Add: assert matcher.Empty == Only and confirm MatchesForSetWithMatcher(_, _, _, _, isEmptyish=true) returns true.
No test covers `&CachePolicyConfig{Empty: CacheEmptyBehaviorAllow}` alone — symmetric coverage of the third Empty enum value.

TestSetDefaults_NetworkConfig_FailsafeMatchMethod (common/defaults_test.go:521) is t.Skip'd (10 subtests). The matchers PR removes the per-method merging the suite tested; the skip is justified, but no replacement coverage was added for how the new matchers-aware defaulting handles per-method overrides interacting with NetworkDefaults.Failsafe / UpstreamDefaults.Failsafe. Add focused tests that exercise: (a) network has empty failsafe, defaults has per-method failsafe → defaults are applied; (b) network has partial failsafe, defaults has full coverage → user policies stand alone (uncovered methods fall through, defaults NOT merged in).
No test verifies failsafe matcher rejection (or silent never-matches) when `Empty` is set on a failsafe (vs cache) matcher — see corr-002.

No test for the 'matcher with empty params constraint vs request with extracted nil params' edge case (matchers/matcher.go:62-82, paramToString(nil) returns ""). Worth a unit test asserting either match=false (current behavior) or whatever the intended semantic is.
